title: Criminal cash to fund phone-free schools as Oxford head’s approach gains backing
summary: Schools across the Thames Valley could receive funding to help keep pupils off mobile phones during the school day, as an Oxford school’s stricter approach attracts wider attention.
body: Schools across the Thames Valley could soon receive funding to help keep mobile phones out of classrooms, as a new initiative backed by the region’s Police and Crime Commissioner builds on an approach already championed by one of Oxford’s best-known headteachers.

The scheme, announced by Police and Crime Commissioner Matthew Barber, will use £255,000 of funding to support secondary schools in Berkshire, Buckinghamshire, Oxfordshire and Milton Keynes to become phone-free during the school day.

Schools will be able to apply for support towards practical measures such as secure storage, including lockable pouches, lockers or boxes, so pupils are unable to access their phones while lessons and school activities are taking place.

The funding is aimed at reducing disruption in classrooms, improving safeguarding and giving pupils a break from online pressures during the school day. According to the PCC, the money comes from criminals, including proceeds from the sale of stolen goods that cannot be returned and court orders made against offenders.

The announcement comes as attention continues to grow around Alastair Chirnside, Warden of St Edward’s School in Oxford, whose stricter phone policy has been credited with improving pupil wellbeing and school life. Mr Chirnside is also set to become Head Master of Eton College, bringing further attention to his approach.

At St Edward’s, younger pupils have faced significant limits on smartphone use during the school week, while the school has also brought back traditional landline telephones in boarding houses to encourage more direct conversations between pupils and their families.

The change has been praised for encouraging pupils to spend more time talking to each other, taking part in sport, reading, and becoming more involved in school activities rather than spending breaks and evenings on their phones.

The Police and Crime Commissioner’s new fund could allow more schools to introduce similar systems without having to meet the full cost themselves. Schools will be expected to use the funding for policies that prevent pupils from accessing and using their phones throughout the whole school day.

The issue has become part of a wider national debate about children’s smartphone use, with concerns raised about distraction, online bullying, peer pressure, harmful content and the effect of screen time on young people’s mental health.

For families and schools across Oxfordshire, the funding marks another step towards phone-free school days becoming more common, while the success of St Edward’s suggests the change can have benefits beyond the classroom.

Secondary schools, middle schools and all-through schools are eligible to apply for the funding, while primary schools, sixth form colleges and private schools are not included in the scheme. Applications are due to close on Sunday 26 July 2026.
author: Witney Wire Newsdesk

title: M40 drivers warned as five sets of roadworks planned over busy weekend
summary: Drivers using the M40 through Oxfordshire are being urged to plan ahead, with several overnight and daytime roadworks scheduled between Friday 27 June and Monday 30 June 2026.
body: Drivers using the M40 through Oxfordshire are being urged to plan ahead after National Highways confirmed a series of planned roadworks affecting both northbound and southbound traffic over the coming days.

Most of the disruption is expected to take place on the evening of Friday 27 June through to the morning of Saturday 28 June, with additional overnight works scheduled between Sunday 29 June and Monday 30 June.

The first planned works begin at 9pm on Friday 27 June, when two of the three southbound lanes will close between junctions 11 and 10. The restrictions will remain in place until 6am on Saturday 28 June.
At the same time, a separate section of the M40 southbound between junctions 10 and 9 will also have lanes one and two closed overnight, with the same 9pm to 6am schedule.

Further south, motorists travelling towards London will face more significant restrictions between junctions 2 and 1A, where three of the four southbound lanes are due to close from 9pm on Friday until 6am on Saturday while planned roadworks are carried out.
Drivers should also be aware of emergency roadworks between junctions 16 and 15 southbound, where disruption is expected between 8am and 6pm on both Friday 27 and Saturday 28 June.
A separate scheme will then affect the M40 northbound between junctions 10 and 12, where overnight roadworks are scheduled from 8pm on Sunday 29 June until 6am on Monday 30 June.

Planned M40 roadworks: Friday 27 June to Saturday 28 June — J11 to J10 southbound: lanes 1 and 2 closed from 9pm to 6am; J10 to J9 southbound: lanes 1 and 2 closed from 9pm to 6am; J2 to J1A southbound: lanes 1, 2 and 3 closed from 9pm to 6am; J16 to J15 southbound: emergency roadworks with disruption expected between 8am and 6pm on both days.
Planned M40 roadworks: Sunday 29 June to Monday 30 June — J10 to J12 northbound: planned overnight roadworks from 8pm until 6am.

Motorists travelling between Oxfordshire, Warwickshire, Buckinghamshire and London are advised to allow extra time for their journeys, particularly during overnight periods when lane closures are in place. Road users should follow signed diversions where applicable and take extra care when travelling through roadwork zones.
As with all planned motorway works, schedules can change at short notice, so drivers are encouraged to check the latest traffic information before setting off.
author: Witney Wire Newsdesk

title: Global electric vehicle company opens new engineering hub in Witney
summary: NIO has opened a new engineering and research facility in Witney, bringing together more than 40 specialist engineers and strengthening Oxfordshire’s role in electric vehicle innovation.
body: A leading global electric vehicle manufacturer has strengthened its commitment to Oxfordshire after opening a new engineering and research facility in Witney, bringing together more than 40 specialist engineers under one roof.
NIO, one of the world’s fastest-growing smart electric vehicle companies, officially opened its new engineering hub as it celebrated 10 years of research and development in the UK.

The new facility consolidates the company’s previous engineering operations into a single base in Witney, creating a central location for advanced vehicle engineering, computer-aided engineering, testing and technology development.
Company leaders say the move reflects NIO’s long-term commitment to the UK and reinforces Oxfordshire’s reputation as one of Europe’s leading centres for automotive engineering and innovation.

The Witney team has played a significant role in developing some of NIO’s most advanced electric vehicles over the past decade, including work on vehicle dynamics, engineering validation and chassis development.
Engineers based in Oxfordshire have also contributed to the company’s EP9 electric supercar and more recent production models sold across international markets.

The new engineering centre spans approximately 9,460 square feet and combines virtual engineering specialists with physical testing facilities. NIO says the expanded site will allow its UK engineers to work even more closely with teams across Europe and China as the company develops future generations of smart electric vehicles.

Hui Zhang, Vice President of NIO Europe, said the Oxfordshire team has become an integral part of the company’s global research and development network over the past decade, contributing to multiple vehicle programmes and engineering innovations.

For Witney, the investment represents another boost for the town’s growing reputation in high-value engineering and technology. Oxfordshire is already home to internationally recognised automotive businesses, Formula One engineering companies and advanced manufacturing organisations, making the county one of the UK’s leading centres for transport innovation.

While many residents will know Witney for its independent businesses and town centre trade, the arrival of a larger engineering operation also highlights the town’s role in attracting international investment and creating highly skilled employment opportunities.

As demand for electric vehicles and sustainable transport technologies continues to grow worldwide, companies such as NIO are increasingly investing in research, engineering and software development to remain competitive.

For local residents considering careers in engineering, software, automotive technology or advanced manufacturing, the continued investment by global companies provides another reminder that internationally recognised opportunities are being created on Witney’s doorstep.
Sources: NIO announcement and industry reporting by The EV Report on the opening of the new Witney engineering facility.
author: Robert

title: Could the next England star come from Witney?
summary: As World Cup excitement builds, local grassroots clubs in Witney could see more young players inspired to pull on their boots.
body: As England’s World Cup campaign captures attention across the country, football clubs in and around Witney could be set for a fresh wave of interest from children inspired by the tournament.

Major international competitions have a way of turning living rooms into mini stadiums, with young fans copying goals, saves and celebrations in gardens, parks and playgrounds the next day. For local clubs, that excitement can be the spark that encourages a child to join a team for the first time.

In Witney, grassroots football is already supported by clubs offering opportunities across a range of ages and abilities. Witney Vikings Youth Football Club says it was established in 1975, has more than 40 teams, and describes itself as the largest youth football club in the Witney area. Source: Witney Vikings Youth Football Club.

Tower Hill Football Club, also based in Witney, says it is an England Football accredited club with youth teams from under-six to under-18 level, as well as women’s, men’s and inclusive teams. The club says its mission is to provide competitive football locally in a safe and controlled environment under FA-qualified coaches. Source: Tower Hill Football Club.

The local picture matters because grassroots football is often where children first learn more than how to pass, shoot or tackle. For many young players, the game helps build confidence, teamwork, discipline and friendships that last well beyond the final whistle.

England Football, the Football Association’s participation platform, encourages families to find local youth football opportunities, including mini-soccer, 9-a-side and 11-a-side football. It also highlights programmes such as Wildcats, aimed at girls aged five to 11, and Comets, a pan-disability football programme for disabled children aged five to 11. Source: England Football.

That wider national push could be important locally if the World Cup encourages more children in Witney to get involved. Clubs depend not only on players, but also on parents, coaches, helpers and volunteers who give up evenings and weekends to keep grassroots football running.

The tournament also offers a reminder that football is not only for children already dreaming of playing for England. Many clubs now provide routes for beginners, girls, boys, disabled children and players who simply want to enjoy being part of a team.
For parents, the message is simple: if the World Cup has sparked an interest, now could be a good time to look at what is available locally.

Whether or not the next England star comes from Witney, the tournament could still leave a lasting legacy if it encourages more children to pull on their boots, join a club and enjoy the game with friends.
For many young players, the journey does not begin in a stadium. It starts on a local pitch, with a volunteer coach, a pair of shin pads and a Saturday morning kick-off.
Sources: Witney Vikings Youth Football Club; Tower Hill Football Club; England Football.
author: Mark

title: Why M&S Café in Witney Might Be the Best Spot in Town for a Coffee Break
summary: From consistently hot coffee to friendly staff and a relaxed atmosphere, the M&S Café in Witney has quietly become one of the town’s most reliable places to stop and recharge.
body: There are plenty of places to grab a coffee in Witney, but the M&S Café inside Marks & Spencer continues to stand out for all the right reasons.
In a time where service can often feel rushed or inconsistent, the café manages to keep things simple: good coffee, friendly staff and a genuinely pleasant atmosphere.
One thing that always stands out is that the coffee is actually hot when it arrives — something that surprisingly isn’t guaranteed everywhere these days.
Whether stopping in for a quick drink while shopping or taking a short break during the day, the café consistently delivers a reliable experience.
The service is also worth mentioning. Staff are regularly welcoming, polite and patient, even during busier periods, which makes the whole place feel far more relaxed than many chain cafés.
The café itself is clean, comfortable and usually calm enough to sit for a while without feeling hurried out the door.
While independent cafés will always play an important role in Witney’s town centre, the M&S Café deserves credit for quietly doing the basics very well — and doing them consistently.
Sometimes that’s exactly what people are looking for.
author: Witney Wire

title: M40 Junction 4 Slip Road Closures Planned Overnight
summary: Drivers using the M40 northbound at Junction 4 are being warned of overnight lane closures due to planned emergency barrier repairs.
body: Motorists are being advised to plan ahead as emergency barrier repairs are scheduled on the M40 northbound entry slip road at Junction 4.
The work is currently listed as pending and is scheduled to begin at 10.15pm on Saturday 13 June, continuing overnight until 6am on Sunday 14 June.
Lane closures will be in place while crews carry out repairs to the safety barrier on the slip road.
Drivers travelling through the area late on Saturday evening and into the early hours of Sunday morning should expect disruption and allow extra time for journeys.
Traffic management measures are expected to remain in place throughout the works. Motorists should follow local signage and signed diversion routes where required.
author: Witney Wire Newsdesk

title: Witney swelters as temperatures hit 34°C
summary: High temperatures have swept across Witney as residents are urged to stay hydrated during the hottest afternoon of the year.
body: Temperatures across Witney climbed to 34°C this afternoon as intense sunshine gripped the town centre.
Despite the unusually hot conditions, the Met Office has not issued any weather warnings for Oxfordshire.
Residents are being advised to drink plenty of water, avoid prolonged sun exposure, and check in on vulnerable neighbours, relatives and friends during the hottest part of the day.
author: Witney Wire Newsdesk

title: Community festival weekend expected to draw crowds
summary: Music, food stalls, family activities and local performers are planned for a busy weekend of events.
body: Organisers say this year's community festival will bring together local businesses, performers, charities and families for a packed programme across the weekend.
author: Events Desk

title: A40 Road Works Cause Delays Around Witney
summary: Drivers are being warned to expect delays on the A40 near Witney due to ongoing road layout works and lane closures in both directions.
body: Motorists travelling through Witney are being advised to allow extra time for journeys after lane closures were introduced on the A40 as part of ongoing construction work to change the road layout.
The affected section is between the B4022 Witney East junction and the A40, A415 and Witney Road junction near the Witney town centre and Ducklington turn off.
Traffic is expected to move slowly in both directions, particularly during peak travel periods, with queues likely while the works remain in place.
Drivers are encouraged to plan ahead, use alternative routes where possible and expect delays while crews continue work on the road layout changes.
author: Witney Wire Newsdesk

title: Roadworks set to affect town centre traffic
summary: Drivers are being warned to allow extra time as resurfacing and maintenance work begins.
body: Temporary traffic management will be in place while essential maintenance is carried out. Residents are advised to check routes before travelling.
author: Travel Reporter

title: Local club celebrates dramatic final victory
summary: A late winner secured silverware after a tense final watched by travelling supporters.
body: The team lifted the trophy after a hard-fought match that stayed level until the closing minutes. Coaches praised the players' resilience and the support from the community.
author: Mark

title: School launches new wildlife garden
summary: Pupils helped plant flowers, herbs and small trees as part of a nature and wellbeing project.
body: Teachers say the project will help children learn about biodiversity, food growing and outdoor wellbeing throughout the school year.
author: Community Reporter

title: Independent café opens with focus on local suppliers
summary: A new café has opened in Witney, offering seasonal food and coffee from regional suppliers.
body: The owners say they want the café to become a friendly meeting place for residents, workers and visitors.
author: Robert

title: Carterton Arts Week gets underway across the town
summary: Workshops, exhibitions and craft sessions are now taking place across Carterton as Arts Week returns for another year.
body: Workshops, exhibitions and craft sessions are now taking place across Carterton as Arts Week returns for another year.
The event runs until 7 June and includes painting sessions, sewing workshops and community arts activities across the town.
Local artists and community groups are taking part throughout the week.
author: Witney Wire Newsdesk
